Guide to Royal Albert

1259 named patterns  ·  928 un-named sets

The Story of Blossom Time

On a spring morning in 1933, G. R. Palmeter left a meeting of the Apple Blossom Committee at the Cornwallis Inn to purchase china from a representative of Royal Albert China of England. While there, he asked the salesman about creating a pattern called “Blossom Time”, to tie in with the Apple Blossom Festival. A design was submitted and the factory got to work on it. The design is an actual picture of the Ralph Eaton farm in the Annapolis Valley — the apple trees at the height of bloom. The result was Blossom Time China, still in great demand all over the world, even as far as Japan, over a period of sixty years. The original plate was square; the factory later tried a round one but it was not well received. Written by G. R. Palmeter, signed and dated June 11, 1992. Held in the Apple Blossom Festival archives at the Kings County Museum, Kentville.

Old Country Roses — Main Collection

The full Old Country Roses pattern, designed by Harold Holdcroft and launched in 1962. Over one hundred million pieces sold worldwide.

Old Country Roses — A Collector's Guide

Written by Ian McCallum  ·  Original eBay Guide

This guide will provide a seller or buyer with enough information to determine that the Old Country Roses china pattern or piece in question is genuine, correctly described, and from approximately when it was manufactured.

History & Design

King's Ransom (1921)
Old Country Roses

This range of English bone china from Royal Albert is renowned the world over, with more than 100 million pieces produced and sold. The Harold Holdcroft inspired Old Country Roses (OCR) was launched in 1962 — originally planned to be called Treasure Garden. Harold Holdcroft MSIA FRSA NRD, Royal Albert's Art Director, based his design on the 1921 pattern King's Ransom.

The design was a result of years of work. Harold Holdcroft said: "We decided to incorporate all the ingredients we knew would be acceptable — richness of colour, softness of colour, good quality and a good value look. It has lush deep red roses, warmth from the tea roses, delicacy from the bluish pink and an overall softening off of the pattern by using a shade of soft warm green. The rich gold stipple trim gives the sprays continuity and the china good value for money."

Enhanced by the elegant traditional Royal Albert Montrose shape and the attractive scalloped edges finished in 22k gold — from the twist and curl of the teacup handles to the fluted trim on the teapot lid.

English Backstamps

Where size allowed, all English produced backstamps consisted of: a floral spray (3 roses — red, yellow and pink on a green leaf background), the pattern name in red, ROYAL ALBERT or ROYAL ALBERT ®, BONE CHINA, ENGLAND or MADE IN ENGLAND, and a copyright line.

1962–1973 (Original)
1973–1993 © ROYAL
1973–1993 © 1962
1973–1993 © ROYAL (detail)
1973–1993 © 1962 (detail)
1993–2002 Royal Albert ®
1993–2002 Royal Albert ® (detail)
1993–2002 Made in England
1993–2002 Made in England (detail)

After checking literally thousands of listings, these five backstamp groups appear to be the only English manufactured range. The first four were used for tableware; the fifth for specialty and gift items.

The Five Backstamp Groups

  • Group 1 — 1962–1972, Original
  • Group 2 — 1973–1993, bottom line: Copyright Royal Albert Ltd 1962
  • Group 3 — 1973–1993, bottom line: Copyright 1962 Royal Albert Ltd
  • Group 4 — 1993–2002, top line: Royal Albert © Registered; bottom: Copyright 1962 Royal Albert Ltd
  • Group 5 — 1993–2002, Made in England (specialty items)

Ian McCallum's view: the original 1962–1972 pieces should be considered most valuable. Harold Holdcroft retired in late 1972 and his attention to detail ensured high quality standards throughout this period.

Factory Seconds

Whenever a product was too good to discard but not good enough to meet first quality standard, the backstamp was scratched or marked, then sold at a discount — usually 50% or more. Common flaws: mis-alignment of graphic transfer, size variation, glazing or gilding flaw, minor impurity or firing pitting, colour variation.

Do not believe a claim that a second is as good as a first. The item became a second for a specific reason. If the seller can't tell you what it is, it just means they can't find it — not that it's not there.

Note: gilders often put a test mark on the back — this does not indicate a flaw.

Asian Production

Production in Asia started as early as 1998. By December 20, 2002, most Royal Albert production including Old Country Roses was transferred to Indonesia, and some to China. Royal Doulton was taken over by Waterford Wedgwood in January 2005 — the sixth owner in OCR's 44-year existence.

Indonesian backstamp
Chinese backstamp
Asian example 1
Asian example 2
Asian example 3
Asian example 4
Asian example 5
Asian example 6
Asian example 7
Asian example 8
Asian example 9
Asian example 10

Indonesian backstamps are mainly detectable by the absence of the word ENGLAND. Chinese backstamps replace "Bone China" with "Made in China." Copyright years on Asian OCR: 1962, 1998, 1999, 2002, 2003, 2004.

More Asian Backstamp Examples

Canada rice bowl
Angel figurine
Pasta bowl — Indonesia
1998, no England
Cameo green plate
Pink lace tea cup
Backstamp bases

The Four Most Common Buying Mistakes

  1. Buying Indonesian or Chinese OCR thinking it is English
  2. Buying 1962 backstamps believing it is the year of manufacture
  3. Buying factory seconds thinking they are first quality
  4. Not checking descriptions for accurate condition of product

Whenever you do not see the word ENGLAND on a backstamp, the item is Asian — most likely Indonesian. Many sellers do not mention the origin of manufacture in their description, so always check the backstamp carefully.

Royal Albert Backstamps — 1894 to Present

Manufactured at T.C. Wild & Sons Limited, Crown China Works, High Street (later St Marys Works), Longton, Staffordshire, England. Royal Albert was the trading name of the firm founded by Thomas Clark Wild, about 1894. The firm has always been known for its fine quality bone china.

c.1896–1904 — Printed or Impressed Crown Marks

The earliest marks used printed or impressed initials, a pattern name, or just a crown. Phased out around 1905. Different coloured backstamps were placed with patterns of similar colour.

TCW mark c.1896
Crown only
Very old crown plate
Daisy pre-1905
Crown only variant
Gem old BS
May old
Poppy old
Rena

1905–1907 — First Use of the "Royal Albert" Name

The second backstamp was the first to carry the name "Royal Albert." Thomas Wild & Co. adopted the Royal Albert name as a result of the Royal Warrant and its many links to the Royal Household. The pattern name, if there was one, was placed on top or inside the backstamp. Sometimes TCW was used or replaced by a pattern name; if the pattern had no name, it was left blank.

1895 — Thomas Wild and Son. Thomas C. Wild bought the Albert Works in Longton, Stoke on Trent. Built in 1846, the Albert Works had been named after Prince Albert, Queen Victoria's Consort.

1897 — Thomas Wild & Co. produced a range of Commemorative Pieces for Queen Victoria's Diamond Jubilee, which led to the presentation of a Royal Warrant.

1905–1907 Patterns
1905 vase
Set 613
Round crest pink
Winnipeg souvenir
Alpha
Doric
Barton
May variant
Florence yellow
Radium
RA backstamp

1907–1922 — Third Backstamp (TCW)

The third backstamp was introduced in 1907. The firm became "& Sons" in 1917, bringing another change in backstamps. Different coloured backstamps continued to be placed with patterns of similar colour.

Third backstamp 1907
Very old backstamp
May pink
Aden

May

1917–1925 — Post WW1 Mark

The firm became T.C. Wild & Sons in 1917 and this Post WW1 mark followed. References to St Mary's Works were used until 1935.

WW1 era mark
TCW & Sons
Different crown
TCW
Lovain
St Mary's Works

1925–1935 — Crown China Backstamps

After TCW was dropped in 1925–1927, several Crown China backstamps appeared. Note the differences in the crowns. Different coloured backstamps continued to be placed with patterns of similar colour.

Regency

The Royal Albert became incorporated as a Limited Company in 1933.

1931–1940 — Bone China Backstamps Begin

In 1935 the backstamp changed again. All references to Crown China Works ceased, and the Bone China theme was taken up. The word "Crown" was swapped for the word "Bone." Between 1931 and 1935 some transitional backstamps used both. Some patterns kept the same backstamp with only those two words swapped; others changed their backstamps entirely.

Crown stamp 1933
Crown 1935 black
Candy Tuft — Crown
NZ Centennial
Early Bone China
Canterbury — Bone
Early Bone China 2
1935–1940
Indian Tree
Old Cathay
Pastella

American Beauty: The Crown China backstamp dates from 1927; the Bone China backstamp from 1941 to 1998.

American Beauty — Crown
American Beauty — Bone

Old English Rose: Changed its backstamp entirely when moving from Crown China to Bone China.

Old English Rose — Crown
Old English Rose — Bone

Petit Point: Dates from 1932; the first backstamp has no reference to Crown China or Bone China. The 1940s backstamp was used until the pattern was discontinued in December 2001.

Petit Point early
Petit Point 1940s

Lady Gay: Produced as Crown China as well as with a backstamp with no reference to either Crown China or Bone China.

Lady Gay Crown

2000–2002 — Last "Made in England" Backstamps

On 20th December 2002, Royal Doulton ceased Royal Albert production in England and moved manufacture to Asia. Backstamps from patterns not "Made in England" do not carry the word "England."

Post-2002 — Asian Production

Production in Asia started as early as 1998. By December 2002, most Royal Albert production including Old Country Roses had transferred to Indonesia, and some to China. Whenever you do not see the word ENGLAND on a backstamp, the item is Asian — most likely Indonesian. Many sellers do not mention origin in their description, so always check the backstamp carefully.

Asian OCR
Rose Confetti — WWRD

The Company is now known as "WWRD" as seen on the new 2012 backstamps. In 2009, Royal Albert was one of three brands to survive the sale of Waterford Wedgwood assets to a venture capital company, following the failure of the parent company in late 2008.

Buyouts and Mergers

Royal Albert's corporate history is long and complicated. Here is the key timeline:

  • 1964 — Rosyln China joined Allied English Potteries
  • 1966 — Shelley China became part of Royal Albert under Allied English Potteries
  • 1960s — The Pearson Group acquired Royal Albert, adding them to Allied English Potteries alongside Paragon
  • 1970 — All connection to founders T.C. Wild & Sons was dropped; the company was renamed Royal Albert Limited
  • 1972 — The Pearson Group acquired Royal Doulton and merged it with Allied English Potteries, bringing in Royal Crown Derby, Royal Albert, Paragon, and the Lawleys chain of shops
  • 1991–1996 — Patterns released with the Royal Albert name added began in 1991; most ceased production by 1996. Colclough and Royal Kent backstamps also included during this period.
  • 1993 — The Royal Doulton Group was "demerged" from the Pearson Group and listed on the Stock Exchange
  • 2002 — English production ceased 20th December
  • 2005 — Waterford Wedgwood completes a £39.9 million takeover of Royal Doulton in February. Nile Street closes 15th April 2005, with 130 workers leaving; approximately 500 jobs lost altogether
  • 2009 — Royal Albert survives the Waterford Wedgwood collapse, continuing under new venture capital ownership as WWRD
Richmond China BS
Richmond Patterns
Richmond BS
FOTM 1970 Royal Albert Ltd
Country Lane — Paragon
Paragon Patterns
Hyde Park — Paragon
Wayside — Colclough
Colclough Patterns
Trentside 1991–1996
Moonlight Rose
Pacific Rose

Richmond China was under the Allied English Potteries group. Many Richmond patterns started as Shelley patterns, then were made under the Royal Albert name using an un-named Royal Albert Bone China backstamp. Only four Richmond patterns had a unique pattern name: "Blue Poppy," "Blue Rock," "Rose Time," and "Wild Anemone." In 1972, the Pearson Group merger absorbed Richmond China patterns under the Royal Albert name, or discontinued them.

Patterns such as Old English Rose ceased shortly after the 1972 merger — about 1977, though nothing definitive has been confirmed. Serena is another pattern that enjoyed enormous success and ceased around this time.

In 1991, Royal Doulton briefly used the Royal Albert name on their hugely successful Beswick Beatrix Potter range of figurines (made under licence from Frederick Warne), but following adverse collector reaction, quickly changed back.

Patterns made exclusively for other companies also carried distinctive backstamps, including those for Hudson's Bay Company, Danbury Mint, and Liverpool Garden Festival.

Amelia
Hudson's Bay Company
Charlotte Jenny Wren
Danbury Mint
Liverpool Garden Festival
New Brunswick Tartan
Viletta's Arts

First Quality vs. Second Quality

Whenever a product was too good to discard but not good enough to meet first quality standard, the backstamp was scratched or marked, then sold at a discount — usually 50% or more. The second has a scratch through the backstamp. Do not believe a claim that a second is as good as a first. The item became a second for a specific reason.

First quality
Second quality — scratched

Misprinted and Backward Backstamps

Occasionally backstamps were printed backwards or contained errors. One backstamp misspelled "China" as "Chnia." The Moonlight Rose copyright year was misprinted as 1962 on some pieces — 1962 being the year Old Country Roses was introduced, not Moonlight Rose (copyright 1987).

Backward backstamp
Backward example 2
Breath of Spring misprint
Serena backward
Friendship Hawthorn bw
Blossom Time bw
Lavender Rose base
"Chnia" spelling error
Val D'or misprint
Moonlight Rose — wrong date
FOTM backward & misprint
Backward example 3
Moss Rose backward

Registered Numbers

Backstamps can provide one indicator of age; registered numbers can provide another. A registered number can be traced to a year of registration in Geoffrey Godden's excellent reference books Encyclopedia of British Porcelain Manufacturers or Encyclopedia of British Pottery & Porcelain Marks under Registered Designs. Note that a pattern was not always registered prior to its release, and some Reg. Nos. are assigned to several patterns.

Reg. number on backstamp
WW1 stamp — Reg. dates 1931
Foxglove Reg. numbers

730452 — Blossom

749633 — Buttercup, Cherry Ripe, Christmas Rose, Clifton, Dainty Dinah, Dorothy, Florette, Foxglove, Happyland, Jazz, Lady Gay, Marlborough, Narcissus, Persian, Rosalie, Russet, Springtime, The Walton, Virginia, Wembley, Woodlawn

1905–1907 Patterns

The oldest known Royal Albert pieces, produced between approximately 1905 and 1907, carrying the earliest form of the Royal Albert name. Many are extremely rare.

Hand Painted Patterns

Patterns from the Crown China era (approximately 1927–1935) whose backstamps explicitly state they were Hand Painted. Many were also produced as Lawley's exclusives.

1905 to 1907 Patterns

Richmond China Patterns

Richmond China was part of the Allied English Potteries group alongside Royal Albert. Many patterns began as Shelley patterns before transferring to Royal Albert. Only four Richmond patterns carried a unique pattern name: Blue Poppy, Blue Rock, Rose Time, and Wild Anemone. After the 1972 Pearson Group merger, remaining Richmond patterns were absorbed under Royal Albert or discontinued.

Paragon Patterns

Paragon China became part of Allied English Potteries alongside Royal Albert in the 1960s. Patterns released with the Royal Albert name added beginning in 1991; most ceased production by 1996.

Colclough Patterns

As Royal Doulton merged with companies including Colclough, some pieces carried backstamps naming both companies. These patterns date primarily from 1991 to 1996.

Cup Shapes

Royal Albert used a number of different cup and saucer shapes over the decades. The same pattern often appeared in several shapes. Knowing the shape name helps identify pieces accurately — and explains why two pieces of the same pattern can look quite different.

The History of Royal Albert

Romantic. Exuberant. Beautiful. Feminine. Floral. These are just some of the descriptions collectors worldwide apply to their favourite brand of bone china tableware and giftware — Royal Albert. A brand inspired by the English country garden and the national flower, the rose.

The Royal Albert Works
The factory
Thomas C. Wild at St Mary's Works

The Founding — 1894 to 1910

The story of Royal Albert stretches back over one hundred years to a small pottery business established by Thomas Wild in 1896, in Longton — one of the six towns that make up Stoke-on-Trent, "The Potteries." This household name began as a family business. For it was the ability and work of Thomas and his sons — Fred and Tom (Thomas Clark, or TC) — that made the company famous for bone china tea and breakfast sets.

The tribute paid to Fred Wild at his death in 1961 could equally apply to all of them: "Much of the success of Royal Albert is a direct result of Fred's courage, foresight and enterprise."

To own a piece of Royal Albert is to have a piece of history in your hands. The household name had links with the Royal Household from the start — after Prince Albert, who became King George VI in 1936. China produced at the factory was initially branded as Albert Crown China. 'Royal' was added in 1904. But it soon became known familiarly as 'Royal Albert.'

The first Royal commemoratives were produced as early as 1897 to celebrate Queen Victoria's Diamond Jubilee. Royal Albert has always offered style that goes beyond the tabletop. In around 1910 the first overseas agency was established in New Zealand, quickly followed by exports to Australia, Canada, and the USA. TC eagerly embraced new technology — Royal Albert was one of the very first in its field to install kilns fired by gas and electricity.

Royal Albert Works sign
Works sign (detail)
Factory signage
Original brochure

Design and the English Style

It was Royal Albert's designers who developed its distinctively English, globally popular style — combining the fineness, whiteness, and purity of the bone china body with sensual, informal rococo shapes and floral designs. The English fondness for cottage gardens and shady woods naturally inspired thousands of designs. Motifs were adapted and updated through period fashions: 1920s vivid Art Deco florals, Victorian chintz, and more. Lady Carlyle, for instance, has proved a popular success for over fifty years.

Many early shapes were fluted and included floral motifs and rich patterns in shades of red, green, and blue in the style of popular Japanese Imari patterns. Royal Albert's early success was linked to an uncanny ability to cater for all tastes — from the modest to the most expensive.

Decorators at work on Royal Albert patterns
Decorators at the factory

Harold Holdcroft and Old Country Roses

When it comes to Royal Albert designers, Harold Holdcroft was probably the most famous of all. He epitomised the team's passion for flowers. Harold designed Old Country Roses — taking both rich and soft colours, he combined lush deep red roses with the warmth of tea roses, alongside the delicacy of blush pink, all softened by a wash of green. He completed the winning design with rich gold stipple and a burnished gold rim. This classic pattern emphasises the full-bodied and curvaceous fluted Montrose tableware shape.

Since its launch in 1962, over one hundred million pieces of Old Country Roses have been sold worldwide — more than any other bone china tableware pattern in history. Harold was succeeded as Art Director by Peter Roberts in 1972.

Harold Holdcroft's signature
Old Country Roses
Old Country Roses — close up
Old Country Roses set

Royal Warrants and Continuing Legacy

Royal Albert has two Royal Warrants and continues to commemorate important Royal events and anniversaries. In 2002, Royal Albert marked both the Golden Jubilee of the accession of Queen Elizabeth II and the death of HM Queen Elizabeth the Queen Mother with small collections.

The brand has always reached beyond tableware. Thanks to a licensee agreement with fashion icon Zandra Rhodes, her "My Favourite Things" range featured her signature butterfly and wiggle across tableware, giftware, and glassware — a quintessentially English appeal that reaches across the globe.

Through the Years — 100 Years of Royal Albert

From Tableware Today

Classic and enduring designs like Old Country Roses, Lady Carlyle, and Moonlight Rose have secured Royal Albert's place in our hearts. In honour of one hundred years of superb English craftsmanship, ten designs were created — each representing a decade in the life of Royal Albert — to commemorate a noble lineage.

1900s — Regency Blue
1910s — Duchess
1920s — Spring Meadow
1930s — Polka Rose
1940s — English Chintz
1950s — Festival
1960s — Golden Roses
1970s — Poppy
1980s — Holyrood

Since 1897, Royal Albert has established its brand as one of the most beloved around the world. Its decidedly English-styled bone china — think of a lovingly tended lush country garden — is honoured in this collection of ten inspired designs, each influenced by historic events and typifying the decade from which it came.

The ten designs span the full century:

  • 1900s — Regency Blue: Rich hues and gold accents signalled the end of Victorian times.
  • 1910s — Duchess: The company's signature roses against a black palette.
  • 1920s — Spring Meadow: Typifying the post-war ebullience and the emergence of a more positive era.
  • 1930s — Polka Rose: Fresh apple green background with a single rose, reflective of the Art Deco period.
  • 1940s — English Chintz: Roses and forget-me-nots against a crackle background with lush gold trim.
  • 1950s — Festival: Blue harebells against a beautiful blue/green border — inspired by the accession of Queen Elizabeth II in 1952.
  • 1960s — Golden Roses: A modish pink background offsets opulent gold roses.
  • 1970s — Poppy: Coral, yellow, and gold, evocative of the decade's flower power.
  • 1980s — Holyrood: A simple burgundy band and gold frieze work, typifying the wealth and entrepreneurship of the era.
  • 1990s — Hartington Lane: Swirling lilacs against a purple background, combining two Royal Albert designs of the decade: Lilac Lane and Hartington.

Royal Albert, at the time of the 100 Years collection, was generating upwards of 40% of parent company Royal Doulton's annual revenue — a testament to the enduring appeal of Old Country Roses alone, which accounted for 80% of Royal Albert's sales. Since its 1962 launch, the total production of Old Country Roses could stretch a mind-boggling 10,000 miles.
